channel.title: NPR Topics: Health
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: Heat, bugs, germs: U.S. public health prepares for the World Cup
item.description: For months ahead of the World Cup, states and cities have been preparing to protect travelers and local communities from potential threats including foodborne illness and infectious diseases.
item.content:encoded: For months ahead of the World Cup, states and cities have been preparing to protect travelers and local communities from potential threats including foodborne illness and infectious diseases.
item.dc:creator: Pien Huang

item.title: Is bovine colostrum really 'liquid gold' for gut health?
item.description: Wellness influencers are promoting supplements of cow colostrum, the first milk that mammals produce after giving birth, for advancing immunity and a healthy gut. Does science back up their advice?
item.content:encoded: Wellness influencers are promoting supplements of cow colostrum, the first milk that mammals produce after giving birth, for advancing immunity and a healthy gut. Does science back up their advice?
item.dc:creator: Ari Daniel

item.title: 5 ways to reduce everyday exposure to 'forever chemicals'
item.description: Mara Hoplamazian has spent years reporting on 'forever chemicals,' or PFAS. Here's what they've learned about what may help limit everyday exposure to the contaminant.
item.content:encoded:
Mara Hoplamazian has spent years reporting on 'forever chemicals,' or PFAS. Here's what they've learned about what may help limit everyday exposure to the contaminant.

(Image credit: show999/Getty Images)
item.dc:creator: Mara Hoplamazian

item.title: Doctors checked Biden just after Trump debate as Jill Biden feared he had a stroke
item.description: The Biden administration previously said doctors examined the president "days" following the debate, not in the moments after. The former first lady revealed more details in her new book.
item.content:encoded:
The Biden administration previously said doctors examined the president "days" following the debate, not in the moments after. The former first lady revealed more details in her new book.

(Image credit: Justin Sullivan)
item.dc:creator: Luke Garrett

item.title: How Ebola kills -- and what it takes to stop it
item.description: It's a virus that can strike with unrelenting force. The kind of care need to knock it out is often not fully available in a lower resource country like the Democratic Republic of Congo.
item.content:encoded:
It's a virus that can strike with unrelenting force. The kind of care need to knock it out is often not fully available in a lower resource country like the Democratic Republic of Congo.

(Image credit: Glody Murhabazi/AFP)
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Lambert

item.title: Michigan found a way to reduce school vaccine waivers. Until it backfired
item.description: A decade ago, Michigan had high rates of parents not vaccinating their children, so it required them to attend an in-person education class to get an exemption. It worked — until things got ugly.
item.content:encoded:
A decade ago, Michigan had high rates of parents not vaccinating their children, so it required them to attend an in-person education class to get an exemption. It worked — until things got ugly.

(Image credit: Kate Wells)
item.dc:creator: Kate Wells

item.title: A cancer vaccine made just for you. mRNA is back and it's fighting melanoma
item.description: A study finds that an mRNA vaccine is highly effective at preventing recurrence of this dangerous skin cancer, when used in combination with Keytruda, an immunotherapy drug.
item.content:encoded: A study finds that an mRNA vaccine is highly effective at preventing recurrence of this dangerous skin cancer, when used in combination with Keytruda, an immunotherapy drug.
item.dc:creator: Allison Aubrey

item.title: Confirmed Ebola cases in Congo near 300 as survivors describe their recoveries
item.description: The outbreak remains focused in Congo's eastern Ituri province. Congo has reported over 1,000 suspected cases with the Bundibugyo virus, which has no approved treatment or vaccine.
item.content:encoded:
The outbreak remains focused in Congo's eastern Ituri province. Congo has reported over 1,000 suspected cases with the Bundibugyo virus, which has no approved treatment or vaccine.

(Image credit: Moses Sawasawa)
item.dc:creator: The Associated Press

item.title: Is bovine colostrum really 'liquid gold' for gut health?
item.description: Baby calves rely on it to build up their immune systems and gut. And now marketers are promoting it for humans. Here's what scientists say.
item.content:encoded: Baby calves rely on it to build up their immune systems and gut. And now marketers are promoting it for humans. Here's what scientists say.
item.dc:creator: Ari Daniel

item.title: How aid cuts are hampering the front-line response to the Ebola crisis
item.description: Aid workers in Uganda are watching an Ebola crisis unfold in neighboring Democratic Republic of Congo. They're doing what they can to prepare for an uptick in cases, but foreign aid cuts aren't helping.
item.content:encoded:
Aid workers in Uganda are watching an Ebola crisis unfold in neighboring Democratic Republic of Congo. They're doing what they can to prepare for an uptick in cases, but foreign aid cuts aren't helping.

(Image credit: Leonard Musinguzi)
item.dc:creator: Henry Larson
